hypothesis: Domain-global frontier namespace disconnection >90% as found in S417
method: Parse all 43 domain FRONTIER.md files and tasks/FRONTIER.md. For each frontier entry, check whether its text block references any frontier ID from the other namespace (global vs domain). Use word-boundary regex matching with self-reference exclusion.
expect: Domain→global linkage <10%, confirming namespace disconnection. Global→domain ~20%.
actual: Domain→global 3.0% (6/201). Global→domain 27.8% (5/18). 38/43 domains have zero global linkage. Bidirectional asymmetry: 9.3x more global→domain than domain→global.
diff: Domain→global worse than predicted (3% vs <10%). Global→domain better (27.8% vs 20%). The asymmetry is novel: global frontiers reference domains 9.3x more often than vice versa. Domain frontiers are written as self-contained within their namespace — they never look up to the global level. Global frontiers occasionally look down to domains for evidence.
conclusion: S417's 95.9% disconnection CONFIRMED at 97.0% (even worse). The namespace gap is structural: domain frontier creation has no mechanism to check or reference global frontiers. Only 5/43 domains have any linkage at all, and those 5 are coincidental (cross-references happened to exist in the text, not from any systematic linking). This is not a routing problem (S417 showed routing is already 79% domain-first) — it's a namespace isolation problem. The two frontier namespaces evolved independently with no bridge mechanism.
